title: A People Without Understanding, Pt. 2
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 04-13-25 -PM- A People Without Understanding Pt 2 - Hosea 4 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Review: Chapter 4 So Far
subpoints: Israel brought to court: charges of no truth, no mercy, no knowledge (Hosea 4:1-3)
The priests are the guiltiest party; a vicious cycle forms between the people and priests
The well-being of God's people depends on Godly, biblically faithful clergy

outline.title: 1. Sin Begets Sin (Hosea 4:11-14)
subpoints: Three sins bundled together: sexual immorality, drunkenness, and idolatry — each leads to the others
We cannot compartmentalize sin; cultivated sin always spills over into other areas of life
The people chose idols because the shaded hillside shrines were more convenient than the journey to Jerusalem
God lays blame squarely on the men of Israel (v.14); the well-being of a church rises and falls with its men

outline.title: 2. Judgment Is Distance from God (Hosea 4:15-19)
subpoints: Judah (representing the righteous) is warned not to participate in Israel's sins
A believer must choose between God and idolatry — cannot have both (Joshua 24; 2 Corinthians 6:14)
Biblical separation from unbelief is not fundamentalism but faithfulness to Scripture
God changes the name of Bethel (house of God) to Beth Aven (house of wickedness)
God's most devastating judgment is to leave us to our own hearts — 'follow your heart' is a judgment of God
Even this withdrawal has a redemptive goal: the wind (Spirit?) in v.19 hints at shame and restoration

summary: An afternoon sermon completing Hosea 4, picking up at verse 11. Two main points are developed. First, sin begets sin (vv. 11-14): the prophet groups together sexual immorality, drunkenness, and idolatry to show that unchecked sin in one area spreads to others — we cannot compartmentalize our sins. Israel chose hillside shrines because they were convenient, illustrating how the lure of spiritual convenience draws people from the true God. God lays blame on the men of Israel (v. 14), and the church only goes as far as its men are willing to go. Second, judgment for sin is God's distancing himself from his people (vv. 15-19): Judah is warned not to be involved in Israel's idolatry; a believer cannot walk hand in hand with unbelief (Joshua 24; 2 Corinthians 6:14). Bethel (house of God) is renamed Beth Aven (house of wickedness). The most devastating divine judgment is simply to let people follow their own hearts. Yet even this withdrawal is redemptive in design — as with the prodigal son, sometimes God must abandon us to ourselves so that we come to our senses. A hint of hope appears in verse 19, suggesting the Spirit is at work even in the judgment.
transcript:
## Review: Hosea 4 So Far

God as the judge of all things brings Israel to court and charges them with many sins that foundationally boil down to no truth, no mercy, and no knowledge. Earlier in chapter 4 we saw the priests — appointed to be God's mouthpiece to the people — are the guiltiest party of all. As a result a vicious cycle begins where the people become more and more like their priests and the priests become more and more like the people. The well-being of God's people is dependent on God's faithful and biblically informed clergy.

This afternoon we finish chapter 4 with two observations: sin begets sin (vv. 11-14), and judgment for sin is often distance from God (vv. 15-19).

## 1. Sin Begets Sin (vv. 11-14)

The more one sins, the more one will sin — that is why radical repentance is so important. A radical turning away from sin and turning to Christ is essential because sin begets sin. Cultivated sin in the heart will give birth to other sins instead of righteousness.

Notice how God links three sins we may not usually group together: sexual immorality, drunkenness, and idolatry. Drunkenness clouds the mind — and so does sexual immorality, and so does idolatry. One leads to the other. We think we can compartmentalize our sins — keep this one in a box over here, and it won't affect anything else. The prophet says no: sin begets sin, they go together, we cannot set boundaries to our sin. It always spills over into other areas of life.

The people who were once guided by God himself in a pillar of cloud or fire now pray to wooden idols. Why? Because sin begets sin. The people who had in their hands the very law of God revealed at Sinai now throw a stick on the ground to get direction from their gods (v. 12). Why? Because sin went unrepented and sin begets sin.

Why did they choose the hillside shrines over Jerusalem? Verse 13 says: because the shade of the trees was good. It was convenient. You did not have to leave home. You did not have to walk all the way to Jerusalem. Brothers and sisters, we live in a culture guided by the principle of convenience — and yet the Bible often portrays worship of God as not the most convenient thing.

Who bears responsibility? Earlier in the chapter, God singled out the priests — but here in verse 14 he lays the blame squarely on the men of Israel. It didn't work for Adam to say 'the woman you gave me,' and it doesn't work now. Whatever blessings and judgments a church experiences will bear some relationship to how sold out its men are for Jesus. The church will only go as far as its men are going.

The paragraph closes with an indictment for today: the lifestyle Israel calls open-minded, thoughtful, and enlightened is actually the result of mindless apostasy (v. 14b). The church in many places brags about being open-minded by embracing sexual immorality — Hosea says that is not open-mindedness, it is mindless apostasy.

## 2. Judgment: Distance from God (vv. 15-19)

Judah here stands for those who worship the true God, and she is warned not to be involved with Israel's sins. A believer must choose between God and idolatry; you cannot have both. This is the challenge Joshua issued in Joshua 24: choose this day whom you will serve. The believer may not even be present when idolatry is taking place.

This call to separation is not popular. You may be called a fundamentalist. But the Bible teaches separation from unbelief — 2 Corinthians 6:14: 'Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers.'

As a sign of judgment, God changes the name of one of the centers of false worship. Bethel — 'house of God' — becomes Beth Aven — 'house of wickedness.' The place that was the house of God, under unbelief, is remade into the house of wickedness.

In verse 16, God will leave Israel to forage on their own. That is the most devastating judgment God can give — to let us do whatever we want. Every movie has one piece of advice: follow your heart. That is a judgment of God when we are left to our own hearts.

Yet even this distance has a goal of reconciliation. Look at verse 19: 'The wind has wrapped her up in its wings and they shall be ashamed because of their sacrifices.' Even God's withdrawal is redemptive. Remember the prodigal son — when did he repent? When he was left to himself, feeding pigs and craving their slop. Then he came to his senses. Sometimes God needs to abandon us for us to come to our senses. The wind — perhaps a reference to the Holy Spirit — is at work even in the judgment to bring back those who belong to God.

## Summary

Chapter 4 has taught us: Israel did not arrive at its wickedness in a vacuum. The wickedness of their hearts, Solomon's backsliding, and Jeroboam's idolatry paved the way. Israel is guilty of no truth, no mercy, no knowledge. The priests who should have been God's mouthpiece are the guiltiest of all. A vicious cycle formed between people and priests. The well-being of the church of God depends on Godly, biblically faithful clergy.

This afternoon we saw that sin begets sin — we cannot compartmentalize willful sin in our lives. And judgment for sin is God distancing himself from us, leaving us to sin. But even then our God is so good: that judgment is designed to bring us back to himself, that we might fellowship with him.
